category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Complex Analysis/Special Functions and Entire Functions

info.text: CSIR NET level MCQ questions on Special Functions: Gamma function, Zeta function, elliptic functions, entire functions, Hadamard factorization, Jensen's formula, Blaschke products.

questiontext.text: The Gamma function \(\Gamma(z)\) is defined for \(\text{Re}(z)>0\) by \(\Gamma(z)=\int_0^\infty t^{z-1}e^{-t}dt\) and extended meromorphically to all of \(\mathbb{C}\) by the relation \(\Gamma(z+1)=z\Gamma(z)\). The poles of \(\Gamma(z)\) are:

feedback.text: From \(\Gamma(z) = \Gamma(z+n+1)/[z(z+1)\cdots(z+n)]\), the poles at \(z=-n\) (for \(n=0,1,2,\ldots\)) are all simple. The residue at \(z=-n\) is \(\lim_{z\to-n}(z+n)\Gamma(z) = \Gamma(1)/[(-n)(-n+1)\cdots(-1)] = (-1)^n/n!\).
The Gamma function has no poles at positive integers; in fact \(\Gamma(n)=(n-1)!\) for positive integers \(n\). The poles are at \(0,-1,-2,\ldots\)
All poles of \(\Gamma\) are simple (order 1). The functional equation gives simple poles at non-positive integers.
The Gamma function has a simple pole at \(z=0\), not an essential singularity.

questiontext.text: The Riemann zeta function \(\zeta(s) = \sum_{n=1}^\infty n^{-s}\) converges absolutely for \(\text{Re}(s)>1\) and extends meromorphically to all of \(\mathbb{C}\) with a simple pole at \(s=1\). The functional equation is \(\zeta(s) = 2^s\pi^{s-1}\sin(\pi s/2)\Gamma(1-s)\zeta(1-s)\). The trivial zeros of \(\zeta\) are at:

feedback.text: From the functional equation: zeros arise when \(\sin(\pi s/2)=0\) (at \(s=-2,-4,-6,\ldots\)) and \(\Gamma(1-s)\) has no poles there. These are the "trivial" zeros. The "non-trivial" zeros lie in the critical strip \(0<\text{Re}(s)<1\), and the Riemann Hypothesis conjectures all lie on \(\text{Re}(s)=1/2\).
At negative odd integers, \(\sin(\pi s/2) = \sin(-k\pi/2)\) where \(k\) is odd, which need not be zero. The zeros occur at negative even integers.

Only at negative EVEN integers is \(\sin(\pi s/2)=0\). At negative odd integers, the sine term is \(\pm1\) (non-zero), but the residues of \(\Gamma\) cancel, actually... Let me verify: \(s=-1\): \(\sin(-\pi/2)=-1\neq0\). So only even negatives are trivial zeros.
The trivial zeros at \(s=-2,-4,-6,\ldots\) are real zeros of \(\zeta\).

questiontext.text: Jensen's formula: if \(f\) is analytic in \(|z|\leq R\) with \(f(0)\neq0\) and zeros \(a_1,\ldots,a_n\) in \(|z|<R\), then \(\log|f(0)| = \sum_{k=1}^n\log\dfrac{|a_k|}{R} + \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}\log|f(Re^{i\theta})|\,d\theta\). This formula is used to:

answer.text: Relate the number of zeros of an entire function to its growth (used in Nevanlinna theory)

feedback.text: Jensen's formula connects the distribution of zeros (the sum \(\sum\log(|a_k|/R)\)) to the boundary behavior of \(\log|f|\). It is the foundation of Nevanlinna value distribution theory and the proof of Hadamard's factorization theorem.

answer.text: Compute the residue of \(f\) at each zero

feedback.text: Jensen's formula involves \(\log|f|\) and is about zero counting, not residues. Residues are for poles.

answer.text: Show that the product of moduli of all zeros equals \(|f(0)|\)

feedback.text: Jensen's formula gives a logarithmic relation, not a product formula. The product formula aspect comes from Blaschke products.

answer.text: Prove that \(f\) is constant if it has infinitely many zeros

feedback.text: By the Identity Theorem, if zeros have a limit point in the domain, \(f\equiv0\). Jensen's formula is about the distribution of zeros, not their isolation.

questiontext.text: A finite Blaschke product \(B(z) = e^{i\theta}\prod_{k=1}^n\dfrac{z-a_k}{1-\bar{a}_kz}\) (with \(|a_k|<1\)) is an automorphism of \(\mathbb{D}\). An infinite Blaschke product \(B(z) = \prod_{k=1}^\infty\dfrac{z-a_k}{1-\bar{a}_kz}\) converges in \(\mathbb{D}\) if and only if:

feedback.text: The Blaschke condition \(\sum(1-|a_k|)<\infty\) is necessary and sufficient for the infinite product to converge uniformly on compact subsets of \(\mathbb{D}\). This characterizes the zero sets of bounded analytic functions on \(\mathbb{D}\) (Hardy space \(H^\infty\)).
Absolute convergence of \(\sum|a_k|\) is too strong a requirement. The Blaschke condition uses \(\sum(1-|a_k|)\), which focuses on how close the \(a_k\) are to the boundary.
Infinite Blaschke products are well-defined when the Blaschke condition holds. They need not be finite sequences.
Note \(1-|a_k|^2=(1-|a_k|)(1+|a_k|)\sim 2(1-|a_k|)\) for \(|a_k|\to1\). So this is equivalent to the Blaschke condition \(\sum(1-|a_k|)<\infty\). Both are equivalent.

name.text: CA29-Q05 Hadamard Factorization — Growth vs Zeros

questiontext.text: Hadamard's theorem: if \(f\) is an entire function of finite order \(\rho\), then \(f(z) = z^m e^{g(z)}\prod_n E_p(z/a_n)\) where \(g\) is a polynomial of degree \(\leq\rho\), and \(p\leq\rho\). The genus of \(f\) is \(\max(p, \deg g)\). For \(\cos(\sqrt{z})\), which grows like \(e^{|\text{Im}(\sqrt{z})|}\) and has zeros at \(z = (2n+1)^2\pi^2/4\):

feedback.text: \(M(r) = \max_{|z|=r}|\cos\sqrt{z}|\approx e^{\sqrt{r}}\) for large \(r\). Then \(\log M(r)\approx\sqrt{r}\), so \(\log\log M(r)\approx\frac{1}{2}\log r\), giving \(\rho=1/2\). This is an entire function of order 1/2.
Order 1 corresponds to exponential growth \(M(r)\sim e^{Cr}\). Here \(\cos\sqrt{z}\) grows like \(e^{\sqrt{r}}\) (much slower), giving order 1/2.
Order 2 would require \(M(r)\sim e^{r^2}\), much faster than \(e^{\sqrt{r}}\). The order is 1/2.
\(\cos\sqrt{z}\) has finite order 1/2. The polynomial \(\cos\sqrt{z}\) does not grow faster than any exponential of a positive power of \(r\).

questiontext.text: A Dirichlet series \(\sum_{n=1}^\infty a_n n^{-s}\) has an abscissa of convergence \(\sigma_c\) and an abscissa of absolute convergence \(\sigma_a\). For the Riemann zeta function \(\zeta(s) = \sum n^{-s}\), these are:

feedback.text: For \(\zeta(s)\): The series \(\sum n^{-s}\) converges conditionally (hence \(\sigma_c\leq1\)) and diverges at \(s=1\) (harmonic series), so \(\sigma_c=1\). Since all terms \(n^{-s}\) are positive for real \(s\), conditional convergence equals absolute convergence: \(\sigma_a=\sigma_c=1\).
The harmonic series \(\sum n^{-1}\) diverges, so \(\sigma_c\geq1\). In fact \(\sigma_c=1\) for \(\zeta(s)\).
While the Riemann Hypothesis concerns the critical line \(\text{Re}(s)=1/2\), the abscissa of convergence is \(\sigma_c=1\). The zeta series diverges at \(s=1\).
The series \(\sum n^{-s}\) diverges for \(\text{Re}(s)\leq1\). The abscissas are both 1, not 0.

questiontext.text: The Weierstrass \(\wp\)-function for a lattice \(\Lambda = \omega_1\mathbb{Z}+\omega_2\mathbb{Z}\) is defined as \(\wp(z) = \dfrac{1}{z^2} + \sum_{\omega\in\Lambda\setminus\{0\}}\left(\dfrac{1}{(z-\omega)^2}-\dfrac{1}{\omega^2}\right)\). The differential equation satisfied by \(\wp\) is:

feedback.text: The Weierstrass \(\wp\)-function satisfies \((\wp')^2 = 4\wp^3 - g_2\wp - g_3\) where \(g_2 = 60\sum_{\omega\neq0}\omega^{-4}\) and \(g_3 = 140\sum_{\omega\neq0}\omega^{-6}\) are the lattice invariants (Eisenstein series \(G_4, G_6\)). This is an elliptic curve equation, establishing a connection between complex tori and elliptic curves.
This is not the correct differential equation. The standard equation for \(\wp\) is a cubic in \(\wp\) on the right side.
This is actually correct (\(\wp'' = 6\wp^2 - g_2/2\)), but the fundamental algebraic relation is \((\wp')^2 = 4\wp^3-g_2\wp-g_3\), which defines the elliptic curve.
This is not a correct identity for the \(\wp\)-function. The correct differential equation is the cubic.

name.text: CA29-Q08 Elliptic Functions — Periods and Liouville

questiontext.text: An elliptic function is a doubly periodic meromorphic function: \(f(z+\omega_1)=f(z+\omega_2)=f(z)\) with \(\omega_1/\omega_2\notin\mathbb{R}\). By Liouville's theorem for elliptic functions, an entire (no poles) elliptic function must be:

feedback.text: An elliptic function with no poles is bounded on the fundamental domain (compact torus), hence bounded everywhere. By Liouville's theorem for entire functions, it must be constant. This is the first Liouville theorem for elliptic functions: every entire elliptic function is constant.
A non-constant polynomial grows unboundedly and cannot be doubly periodic. Elliptic functions must be either constant or have poles.
A non-zero constant is also an entire elliptic function. The conclusion is "constant," which could be any non-zero value.
The \(\wp\)-function has double poles at lattice points, so it is not entire. The answer for entire elliptic functions is that they must be constant.

questiontext.text: The modular group \(\text{PSL}(2,\mathbb{Z})\) acts on the upper half-plane by \(\tau\mapsto(a\tau+b)/(c\tau+d)\) with \(a,b,c,d\in\mathbb{Z}\), \(ad-bc=1\). The fundamental domain for this action has area \(\pi/3\) in the hyperbolic metric. The j-invariant \(j(\tau)\) is a modular function of weight 0 that:

answer.text: Classifies elliptic curves up to isomorphism: \(j(\tau_1) = j(\tau_2)\) iff \(\tau_1\) and \(\tau_2\) are in the same \(\text{PSL}(2,\mathbb{Z})\) orbit

feedback.text: The \(j\)-invariant is a complete invariant for isomorphism classes of elliptic curves over \(\mathbb{C}\). Two tori \(\mathbb{C}/\Lambda_1\) and \(\mathbb{C}/\Lambda_2\) are isomorphic iff their periods are related by a modular transformation, iff their \(j\)-invariants are equal.

answer.text: Counts the number of lattice points inside a fundamental domain

feedback.text: The \(j\)-invariant is a holomorphic function classifying elliptic curves, not a counting function for lattice points.
The \(j\)-invariant takes all complex values and is unbounded. It has a simple pole at \(\tau=i\infty\) with \(j\sim e^{-2\pi i\tau}\) as \(\tau\to i\infty\).

answer.text: Is related to the Riemann zeta function by \(j(\tau)=\zeta(\tau)\)

feedback.text: The \(j\)-invariant and Riemann zeta function are unrelated. The \(j\)-invariant is defined via the Eisenstein series as \(j(\tau) = 1728g_2^3/(g_2^3-27g_3^2)\).

questiontext.text: An infinite product \(\prod_{n=1}^\infty(1+a_n)\) converges absolutely if and only if \(\sum_{n=1}^\infty|a_n| < \infty\). For the product \(\prod_{n=1}^\infty\cos(z/2^n)\), which telescopes to \(\sin(z)/z\) via the identity \(\sin(2\theta)=2\sin\theta\cos\theta\), the convergence holds:

answer.text: For all \(z\in\mathbb{C}\) (the product converges to \(\sin z/z\) everywhere)

feedback.text: By the duplication formula: \(\sin z = 2^n\sin(z/2^n)\prod_{k=1}^n\cos(z/2^k)\). As \(n\to\infty\), \(2^n\sin(z/2^n)\to z\), giving \(\sin z/z = \prod_{k=1}^\infty\cos(z/2^k)\). This converges for all \(z\) (including \(z=0\) by the limit convention \(\sin z/z\to1\)).
The product converges for all \(z\in\mathbb{C}\), not just \(|z|<\pi\). This is an identity valid everywhere.
The identity extends to all complex \(z\). The telescoping argument works in \(\mathbb{C}\).
The product converges to \(\sin z/z\) for all \(z\neq0\) (and to 1 at \(z=0\) by continuity).

questiontext.text: The Beta function is \(B(x,y) = \int_0^1 t^{x-1}(1-t)^{y-1}dt = \dfrac{\Gamma(x)\Gamma(y)}{\Gamma(x+y)}\). Evaluate \(B(3/2, 3/2)\):

feedback.text: \(\Gamma(3/2)^2 = \pi/4\) but we divide by \(\Gamma(3)=2\), giving \(\pi/8\), not \(\pi/4\).
The computation gives \((\sqrt{\pi}/2)^2/\Gamma(3) = (\pi/4)/2 = \pi/8\), not \(3\pi/8\).

questiontext.text: The Poisson integral formula for the unit disk: if \(u\) is harmonic in \(|z|<1\) and continuous on \(|z|\leq1\), then \(u(re^{i\theta}) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}\dfrac{1-r^2}{1-2r\cos(\theta-\phi)+r^2}u(e^{i\phi})d\phi\). The kernel \(P_r(\theta-\phi) = \dfrac{1-r^2}{1-2r\cos(\theta-\phi)+r^2}\) is the Poisson kernel. As \(r\to1^-\), \(P_r(\theta-\phi)\):

feedback.text: For fixed \(\theta\neq\phi\), \(P_r(\theta-\phi)\to0\) as \(r\to1\). At \(\phi=\theta\), \(P_r(0)=(1+r)/(1-r)\to\infty\). The total integral \(\frac{1}{2\pi}\int P_r = 1\) for all \(r\). So \(P_r(\theta-\phi)/(2\pi)\) approximates a Dirac delta, confirming \(u(re^{i\theta})\to u(e^{i\theta})\) as \(r\to1\) (non-tangentially).
If the Poisson kernel converged to a constant, the integral would be the average of \(u\) on the boundary (not the boundary value at \(\theta\)). The kernel peaks near \(\phi=\theta\) as \(r\to1\).
The Poisson kernel integrates to \(2\pi\) (so the normalized version \(P_r/(2\pi)\) integrates to 1), but it doesn't become identically 1 as \(r\to1\).
While \(P_r(\theta-\phi)\to0\) for \(\phi\neq\theta\), it diverges at \(\phi=\theta\). The mass concentrates at a point, not vanishes.

questiontext.text: The Hardy space \(H^2(\mathbb{D})\) consists of analytic functions \(f(z)=\sum_{n=0}^\infty a_nz^n\) in \(\mathbb{D}\) with \(\|f\|^2 = \sum_{n=0}^\infty|a_n|^2 < \infty\). The boundary values satisfy \(\|f\|_{H^2}^2 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}|f(e^{i\theta})|^2d\theta\). Which is NOT in \(H^2(\mathbb{D})\)?

feedback.text: \(\|f\|^2 = \sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{1}{n+1} = \sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{1}{n} = \infty\) (harmonic series diverges). So this \(f\notin H^2\). Note \(f\) is analytic in \(\mathbb{D}\) but its boundary \(L^2\) norm is infinite.
\(e^z = \sum z^n/n!\), so \(\|e^z\|^2=\sum1/(n!)^2<\infty\). So \(e^z\in H^2(\mathbb{D})\).

questiontext.text: The Gauss multiplication formula for the Gamma function is \(\prod_{k=0}^{n-1}\Gamma\!\left(z+\dfrac{k}{n}\right) = \dfrac{(2\pi)^{(n-1)/2}}{n^{nz-1/2}}\Gamma(nz)\). For \(n=2\), this gives the duplication formula. Evaluate \(\Gamma(1/4)\Gamma(3/4)\):

feedback.text: By the reflection formula: \(\Gamma(1/4)\Gamma(1-1/4) = \Gamma(1/4)\Gamma(3/4) = \frac{\pi}{\sin(\pi/4)} = \frac{\pi}{1/\sqrt{2}} = \pi\sqrt{2}\).
\(\Gamma(1/2)\Gamma(1/2)=[\Gamma(1/2)]^2=\pi\), but \(\Gamma(1/4)\Gamma(3/4)=\pi/\sin(\pi/4)=\pi\sqrt{2}\neq\pi\).
\(\pi/\sin(\pi/4)=\pi/(1/\sqrt{2})=\pi\sqrt{2}\neq\sqrt{2\pi}\). These differ by a factor of \(\sqrt{\pi}\).
\(\Gamma(1/4)\Gamma(3/4) = \pi/\sin(\pi/4) = \pi/(1/\sqrt{2}) = \pi\sqrt{2}\), not \(\pi/\sqrt{2}\).

questiontext.text: Stirling's formula: \(\Gamma(z+1) \sim \sqrt{2\pi z}(z/e)^z\) as \(|z|\to\infty\) in \(|\arg z|<\pi\). More precisely: \(\Gamma(z+1) = \sqrt{2\pi z}(z/e)^z e^{\theta/(12z)}\) for some \(\theta\in(0,1)\). Using this, the ratio \(\Gamma(n+1)/\Gamma(n)\) equals:

feedback.text: This follows directly from the functional equation: \(\Gamma(n+1) = n\cdot\Gamma(n)\), so \(\Gamma(n+1)/\Gamma(n) = n\) exactly. No Stirling approximation is needed for this ratio.
The ratio is exactly \(n\) by the functional equation \(\Gamma(z+1)=z\Gamma(z)\), not approximately \(n/e\).
This would suggest \(\Gamma(n+1)\) grows like \(\Gamma(n)\times\sqrt{n}\). The exact ratio is \(n\), not \(\sqrt{n}\).
For integer \(n\), \(\Gamma(n+1)=n!\) and \(\Gamma(n)=(n-1)!\). So the ratio is \(n!/( n-1)!=n\), confirming the answer is \(n\).

questiontext.text: The Gauss hypergeometric function \({}_2F_1(a,b;c;z) = \sum_{n=0}^\infty \dfrac{(a)_n(b)_n}{(c)_n n!}z^n\) where \((a)_n=a(a+1)\cdots(a+n-1)\) is the Pochhammer symbol. It converges for \(|z|<1\). Which of the following is NOT an elementary function that is a special case of \({}_2F_1\)?

feedback.text: Bessel functions are NOT special cases of the Gauss hypergeometric function \({}_2F_1\). They are special cases of the confluent hypergeometric function \({}_1F_1\) (Kummer's function). The confluent limit occurs when two singularities of \({}_2F_1\) merge to form an essential singularity.
Legendre polynomials are indeed special cases of \({}_2F_1\) with integer \(a=-n\) (terminating the series).
\({}_2F_1(a,1;1;z)=\sum\frac{(a)_n(1)_n}{(1)_nn!}z^n=\sum(a)_nz^n/n!=\sum\binom{a+n-1}{n}(-1)^n(-z)^n=(1-z)^{-a}\) by the binomial theorem. A valid special case.

questiontext.text: The winding number \(n(\gamma, a) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi i}\int_\gamma\dfrac{dz}{z-a}\) can be computed for piecewise smooth curves. For the figure-eight curve that winds once counterclockwise around \(a=1\) and once clockwise around \(a=-1\), the winding numbers are:

feedback.text: The figure-eight winds counterclockwise around \(a=1\) (winding number \(+1\)) and clockwise around \(a=-1\) (winding number \(-1\)). By the residue theorem, \(\int_\gamma f\,dz = 2\pi i[n(\gamma,1)\text{Res}(f;1) + n(\gamma,-1)\text{Res}(f;-1)]\).
The two loops of the figure-eight have opposite orientations. One clockwise gives winding number \(-1\), not \(+1\).
Zero winding numbers would mean both points are outside or the loops are trivial. For a figure-eight winding around both points, neither winding number is zero.
The figure-eight winds once (not twice) around each point, and the orientations are opposite, giving \(+1\) and \(-1\).

name.text: CA29-Q18 Cartan's Theorem on the Disc

questiontext.text: The Cartan uniqueness theorem: if \(f:\mathbb{D}\to\mathbb{D}\) is analytic with \(f(0)=0\) and \(f'(0)=1\), then \(f(z)=z\) (the identity). This follows from the Schwarz lemma. More generally, if \(f: D\to D\) (bounded domain) is analytic with \(f(z_0)=z_0\) and \(f'(z_0)=I\), then:

feedback.text: Cartan's theorem (Cartan 1931): if \(f:D\to D\) is a holomorphic map with a fixed point \(z_0\) where \(f'(z_0) = \text{id}\) (identity derivative), and \(D\) is a bounded domain, then \(f = \text{id}\). This is proved using the Schwarz lemma applied to iterates of \(f\).
On the unit disk, automorphisms are Möbius transformations. But Cartan's theorem gives a stronger conclusion: \(f\) must be the identity, not just any Möbius transformation.
With \(f'(z_0)=I\neq0\), \(f\) is non-constant. The conclusion is \(f=\text{identity}\), not constancy.
Cartan's theorem gives a precise conclusion: \(f=\text{id}\). This is a rigidity theorem for holomorphic self-maps of bounded domains.

name.text: CA29-Q19 Order and Type of Entire Functions

questiontext.text: An entire function of finite order \(\rho\) and finite type \(\sigma\) satisfies \(|f(z)|\leq Ae^{\sigma|z|^\rho}\). The Borel-Carathéodory theorem relates the maximum of \(|f|\) and \(\text{Re}(f)\). For \(f(z) = e^{z^n}\) (n a positive integer), the order and type are:

feedback.text: \(|e^{z^n}| = e^{\text{Re}(z^n)} \leq e^{|z|^n}\). So \(M(r) = e^{r^n}\). \(\log M(r)=r^n\), so \(\rho=n\). The type: \(\sigma = \limsup_{r\to\infty}(\log M(r))/r^\rho = \lim_{r\to\infty}r^n/r^n = 1\). Order \(n\), type 1.
The type is \(\limsup(\log M(r))/r^n = r^n/r^n = 1\), not \(n\).
\(M(r)=e^{r^n}\) implies order \(n\) (not 1). Order 1 would correspond to \(M(r)\sim e^{Cr}\).
The order of \(e^{z^n}\) is \(n\) (by computing \(\log\log M(r)/\log r = \log(r^n)/\log r = n\)), not \(n^2\).

questiontext.text: Runge's approximation theorem: if \(K\subset\mathbb{C}\) is compact and \(\mathbb{C}\setminus K\) is connected, then every function analytic in a neighborhood of \(K\) can be uniformly approximated on \(K\) by polynomials. If \(\mathbb{C}\setminus K\) is not connected, we can approximate by rational functions with poles in each bounded component of \(\mathbb{C}\setminus K\). The condition "\(\mathbb{C}\setminus K\) connected" is equivalent to:

answer.text: \(K\) has no holes (K is polynomially convex: \(\hat{K}=K\))

feedback.text: The polynomial hull \(\hat{K} = \{z: |p(z)|\leq\max_K|p|\text{ for all polynomials }p\}\) equals \(K\) iff \(\mathbb{C}\setminus K\) is connected (no bounded components). This polynomial convexity is exactly what enables polynomial approximation. A disk with a hole removed would fail this condition.

answer.text: \(K\) is a convex set (in the geometric sense)

feedback.text: Geometric convexity implies polynomial convexity but is much stronger. Many non-convex sets (like circles, arcs) are polynomially convex.
Domains are open, but \(K\) is compact (closed and bounded). "Simply connected" for compact sets means \(\mathbb{C}\setminus K\) is connected, which is the correct condition.
Finite point sets are polynomially convex, but the condition is more general — it includes all compact sets with connected complement.
